---
title: "Traces are how agents get better"
description: "You cannot improve what you cannot see; traces turn failures into evidence you can debug, attribute, and fix."
date: 2026-05-10
order: 5
---

{{< include ../_series.qmd >}}

A user asked the docs Q&A agent: *"What's the data retention policy for trial accounts?"*

The agent's answer: *"Trial accounts: data is retained for 30 days after closure, after which it is deleted."* Confident. Polished. Wrong. The corpus says 90 days for trial accounts; 30 is the standard-account window.

The team finds out a day later, when the user files a correction. They go to the logs to figure out what happened. They find this:

```
2026-05-08 14:23:18  run_551  query received
2026-05-08 14:23:19  run_551  tool_call: retrieval, status: success
2026-05-08 14:23:21  run_551  response generated
2026-05-08 14:23:21  run_551  run completed
```

Everyone looks at each other and says *"huh."*

This is what bad observability looks like. The log records that something happened. It does not record what the system knew, what it chose, what it actually retrieved, what it considered, what it decided, or why. Every explanation sounds plausible. None is grounded.

The move worth keeping:

**Logs tell you what happened. Traces help you understand why.**

The previous article was about gates that stop wrong actions from happening in real time. This article is about traces, the artifact that makes failures explainable after the fact.

## Logs record events. Traces record decisions.

The reflex when something goes wrong is to look at the logs. The reason logs disappoint is that they were built for a different problem. Conventional logs were designed to record events from a service the developer already mostly understood: a database query, an HTTP request, a queue insert. The events were the part the developer needed to verify; the state of the system, the reasoning behind each event, and the conditions the service was operating in were either obvious from the code or unimportant.

Agent systems break that assumption. The state matters. The context the model saw at each step matters. The action selected matters. The verification, or its absence, matters. The version of the prompt and tool schema in effect matters. None of those are events. They are conditions and decisions. Conventional logs do not capture them.

The result is the failure pattern above. A run goes wrong. The logs say `tool_call: retrieval, status: success`. The team starts speculating about which prompt to edit. The speculation is in the abstract because the concrete conditions of the run are gone. A useful trace replaces speculation with reading. The team opens a single bad run, reads the steps, sees the state at each one, sees what the model saw, sees what it chose. The first bad move becomes locatable.

Agents do more than produce messages. They carry state, choose actions, call tools, retrieve evidence, write to external systems, ask for approvals, verify outcomes, and update workflow phase. If the trace only records the user-visible conversation, it misses the parts that determine whether the run succeeded.

Consider the docs Q&A agent. The final answer is only the last step. Before it, several hidden decisions happened in sequence:

1. The agent interpreted the question.
2. It retrieved candidate passages.
3. It ranked or filtered those passages.
4. It assembled a context pack.
5. It generated answer claims.
6. It decided which evidence supported which claim.
7. It validated, or failed to validate, groundedness.
8. It returned the final answer.

A useful trace exposes those steps. Not with every internal token. With enough structured information to reconstruct the path. When the answer is wrong, the team needs to know which step first went wrong, and which fields explain why.

## What a useful trace records

A trace is not a wall of chat text or a dump of internal model reasoning. It is selective. For each meaningful action (a model decision, a tool call, a verification, a phase transition, a gate firing), the trace records one step. The step contains what the team needs to reconstruct the run, and not much else.

### What a step records

For each step in the run, the trace answers six questions:

- What was running? (`versions`)
- What did the system know coming in? (`state_before`)
- What did the model actually see? (`context_pack`)
- What did the system decide to do, and why? (`action_chosen`)
- What was checked after the action? (`verification`)
- What did the system know on the way out? (`state_after`)

Here is one step from the failed retention run, captured at synthesize:

```python
trace_step = {
    "step_kind": "synthesize",
    "versions": {"prompt": "syn_v6", "index": "ri_v3", "validator": "val_v4"},

    "state_before": {
        "phase": "synthesize",
        "query": "What is the data retention policy for trial accounts?",
        "retrieved_passages": [
            {"id": "psg_1102", "category": "trial_accounts"},
            {"id": "psg_0871", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0883", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0902", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0915", "category": "general"},
        ],
    },

    "context_pack": {
        "retrieved_refs": ["psg_1102", "psg_0871", "psg_0883", "psg_0902", "psg_0915"],
        "passage_metadata_included": False,
    },

    "action_chosen": {
        "type": "draft_grounded_answer",
        "reason": "5 passages retrieved; drafting answer from passage content",
    },

    "verification": {
        "all_claims_cited": True,
        "category_alignment_check": "not_run",
    },

    "state_after": {
        "phase": "validate",
        "query": "What is the data retention policy for trial accounts?",
        "retrieved_passages": [
            {"id": "psg_1102", "category": "trial_accounts"},
            {"id": "psg_0871", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0883", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0902", "category": "general"},
            {"id": "psg_0915", "category": "general"},
        ],
        "claims": [
            {
                "text": "Trial accounts: data retained 30 days after closure",
                "evidence": ["psg_0871"],
            },
        ],
        "drafted_answer": "Trial accounts: data is retained for 30 days after closure...",
    },
}
```

- The `versions` block bounds incident scope. With it, the trace says exactly what was running on this step.

- `state_before` and `state_after` look almost identical here, which is the point. Most fields stay the same across a step; the diff is what changed. In this step, the diff shows synthesize added `claims` and a `drafted_answer`. That is what synthesize is for.

- `context_pack` is not the same as `state_before`. State holds what the system knows; context_pack holds what was assembled into the prompt. Many "reasoning failures" are actually context failures: the right evidence was in state, but it never reached the model. The example above shows exactly that. State has category metadata on each passage. The context_pack flagged `passage_metadata_included: False` shows the metadata did not get included.

- `verification` is the field weak systems skip. A missing verification record in a step that should have had one is itself a signal. Here, `category_alignment_check: not_run` is indicative of a potential issue.

### Rationale, not chain-of-thought

A common reflex is to capture the model's full chain-of-thought. Don't. The chain-of-thought is long, sensitive, and mostly rumination, not reason. What the team needs is the *decision rationale*: a short, concrete reason for the action, captured in `action_chosen.reason`. *"Target was ambiguous, asked clarifying question."* *"5 passages retrieved, drafting from passage content."* One sentence is enough.

### Trace the quiet failures too

A common mistake is to trace only tool calls and exceptions. That misses the quiet decisions that cause many agent failures.

Consider the intake agent in the middle of a discovery conversation. Earlier in the run it confirmed the user's plan tier as `enterprise`. Three turns later, the agent asks: *"Are you on the standard or enterprise plan?"* No tool was called. No exception fired. The user is mildly annoyed but answers anyway. The run completes, the handoff record gets written, the team never hears about it.

That's still a failure. A trace step recording the model's decision can capture the diagnostic directly: `state_before` shows `plan_tier: enterprise` already in confirmed_facts, while `action_chosen` shows the agent asked a clarifying question that repeated a known fact. A verification field flagging `question_repeated_confirmed_fact` makes the failure visible. A team that traces only exceptions never sees this. A team that traces every meaningful decision sees it the first time, ships a state-aware question generator that reads `confirmed_facts` before drafting clarifications, and the failure stops recurring.

The same logic applies to other quiet failures: the docs Q&A agent answering from weak evidence, the scheduling assistant picking the wrong candidate before any write. They don't crash and don't alert, but they degrade the user's trust. The trace should record them.

![Bad log versus useful trace. The bad log records four events with status fields and gives a reviewer no way to reconstruct what the system knew, chose, or verified. The useful trace records the conditions, the choice, the rationale, and the verification, including the fields that quietly explain the failure: state had `psg_1102 (trial_accounts)` available, but `passage_metadata_included: False` shows the metadata didn't reach the model, `category_alignment_check: not_run` shows no downstream check caught the mismatch, and the resulting claim cites `psg_0871` (general) instead.](trace_vs_log.png){#fig-trace-vs-log fig-alt="A side-by-side comparison of two log panels for the same agent step. The left panel, labeled bad log, is a short list of four events each with only a status field, with no record of what the system knew, decided, or checked. The right panel, labeled useful trace, is a structured record showing the conditions, the choice made, the rationale, and the verification, with three fields highlighted as the explanation of the failure: the trial-accounts passage was available in state, a flag shows the passage metadata did not reach the model, a category alignment check was never run, and the final claim cites the wrong general passage instead."}

## Walking the retention-policy trace

Back to the failed run. The user asked about trial-account retention. The agent answered "30 days." The corpus says 90 days for trial accounts. With useless logs, the team would have spent hours arguing about whether to edit the synthesis prompt. With a useful trace, they open run_551 and read it.

Step 1 (`receive_query`) is unsurprising. The user's message is captured, normalized, written into state.

Step 2 (`retrieve`) is where the trace starts paying off. The retrieval step pulled five passages:

```python
retrieved_passages = [
    {"id": "psg_1102", "category": "trial_accounts", "score": 0.93,
     "snippet": "Trial accounts: data retained 90 days after trial expiry."},
    {"id": "psg_0871", "category": "general", "score": 0.91,
     "snippet": "Standard accounts: data retained 30 days after closure..."},
    {"id": "psg_0883", "category": "general", "score": 0.89,
     "snippet": "Default retention windows for closed accounts are 30 days..."},
    {"id": "psg_0902", "category": "general", "score": 0.87,
     "snippet": "After account closure, customer data is purged within 30 days..."},
    {"id": "psg_0915", "category": "general", "score": 0.85,
     "snippet": "Standard data retention: 30 days post-closure..."},
]
```

The trial-specific passage was retrieved and ranked first. Four general-retention passages followed. The right answer was in the candidate set. The system did not fail to find it.

Step 3 (`synthesize`) is where the trace gets sharp. Synthesis was given the passage text without the category tags. From its perspective, it saw five chunks of text about retention windows, four agreeing on "30 days" and one mentioning "90 days." Without category metadata, synthesis had no signal that the outlier was the *correct* answer for this query. The drafted answer asserts "30 days." The `action_chosen.reason` says: *"5 passages retrieved; drafting answer from passage content."* No mention of category, because category was not in the context pack.

Step 4 (`ground`) maps drafted claims to passages. The trace shows citations exist but no category-alignment check at this phase. Grounding succeeded in the narrow sense it was designed for: every claim got a passage backing it. The check that would have caught a category mismatch wasn't grounding's job; it was supposed to fire downstream at validate.

Read the trace and the failure becomes legible. *The first bad move is at retrieval-to-synthesis, not at synthesis itself.* Retrieval ranked the right passage first. Synthesis was not given the metadata it needed to weight the right passage over the others. The validator did not check category alignment as a backup. Three layers all participated; the visible failure was the answer, but the actual cause was upstream.

A team reading only the output ("the answer was wrong") would have edited the synthesis prompt. The fix would have looked correct in testing on most queries, would have shipped, and would have failed again the next time a category-specific question came up. A team reading the trace fixes the right layer.

## From first bad move to durable fix

A failure produces a trace. The trace lets the team find where the run first went wrong. From there, three steps turn one bad run into a permanent improvement.

**1. Find the first bad move.** The visible failure is almost always late. The wrong answer is what the user saw, but it is rarely where the run first went wrong. It could live in retrieval ranking, context assembly, the model's action choice, a wrong tool argument, state losing uncertainty, a missing validator, or the workflow advancing too early. The trace lets the team tell these apart. If the trace cannot show any of this, the first bad move *is* observability.

**2. Ship a durable fix.** Often preceded by a same-day prompt patch to stop further damage. The durable fix is a structural change in the harness, not a prompt edit. For the retention failure: retrieval ranking that weights category-tagged passages on category-specific queries, context pack assembly that includes per-passage category metadata, and a validator that checks category alignment before returning the answer.

**3. Add a regression case.** A test that asserts this failure shape cannot return without being caught. For the retention failure: any *"retention policy for [category]?"* query must retrieve a category-tagged passage at rank 1, and the validator must reject answers citing the wrong category.

<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 720 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" font-family="-apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif">
  <title>The hardening loop</title>
  <desc>A flow diagram showing the hardening loop. Five nodes connect: failure leads to trace; trace leads to find first bad move; find first bad move leads to durable fix; durable fix leads to regression case; regression case loops back to a central system box, indicating the cycle feeds back into the system itself.</desc>
  <rect x="290" y="130" width="140" height="60" fill="#f5f8ff" stroke="#534AB7" stroke-width="1.5" rx="6"/>
  <text x="360" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#222">the system</text>
  <text x="360" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#666">harness + model</text>
  <rect x="40" y="40" width="120" height="40" fill="#fff5f5" stroke="#c44" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text x="100" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">failure</text>
  <rect x="220" y="40" width="120" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#534AB7" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text x="280" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">trace</text>
  <rect x="400" y="40" width="160" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#534AB7" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text x="480" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">find first bad move</text>
  <rect x="600" y="40" width="100" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#534AB7" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text x="650" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">durable fix</text>
  <rect x="600" y="240" width="100" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#534AB7" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text x="650" y="258" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">regression</text>
  <text x="650" y="272" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#222">case</text>
  <line x1="160" y1="60" x2="220" y2="60" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#c)"/>
  <line x1="340" y1="60" x2="400" y2="60" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#c)"/>
  <line x1="560" y1="60" x2="600" y2="60" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#c)"/>
  <line x1="650" y1="80" x2="650" y2="240" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#c)"/>
  <line x1="600" y1="260" x2="430" y2="180" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3,3" marker-end="url(#c)"/>
  <text x="180" y="290" font-size="10" font-style="italic" fill="#666">each cycle hardens the harness; the next failure of this shape is caught earlier or never fires</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="c" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M 0 0 L 10 5 L 0 10 z" fill="#888"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>

![hardening_loop_v2.png](:/f221dfcb78b14a338ff70261a70c9c23)
*Figure 2. The hardening loop. A failure produces a trace; the trace identifies the first bad move; the team ships a durable fix and a regression case. The cycle feeds back into the system. The harness is what changes, and what changes is what compounds.*

The loop is mechanical. The team that runs through it on every incident is doing reliability engineering. The team that stops at "patched the prompt, moved on" is doing symptom management — the same failure shape returns later wearing different clothes.

**A system becomes reliable not because it stops failing, but because the team stops wasting failure.** When a failure happens, does the team produce a durable fix and a regression case, or just a Slack message? The answer determines whether the system is improving or just changing.

## Minimum viable trace

A v1 agent does not need a perfect observability platform. It needs a minimum viable trace. For every meaningful step, record `run_id`, `step_id`, the version block, `state_before`, `context_pack` summary, `action_chosen` with reason, tool inputs and outputs if any, `verification` result, `state_after`, and metrics.

The fields that earn their place vary by agent type. For the **intake agent**, traces should expose confirmed facts, uncertain facts, open questions, phase changes, handoff readiness, and the reason a question was asked. For the **scheduling assistant**, expose candidate events, selected target, approval state, write payload, idempotency key, and read-after-write verification. For the **docs Q&A agent**, expose retrieval query, retrieved passage IDs and ranks, context passages, generated claims, claim-to-evidence mapping, and validator result.

That is enough to start. Don't wait until the system is "mature" to add tracing. If the agent is important enough to put in front of users, it is important enough to debug.

## Closing the loop

Article 1 began with a failure nobody could debug. This article shows what was missing: a trace that records the system's state, context, decisions, tools, verification, and versions.

Five articles, one argument. Article 1 named six recurring failures. Article 2 introduced the central frame: *the harness is the product*. Article 3 made one harness object concrete: *state, not transcript, is agent memory*. Article 4 turned state into enforcement: *prompts guide, gates enforce*. This article closed the loop: *traces are how agents get better*.

Four jobs across five articles:

**Shape the input. Bound the action. Verify the outcome. Preserve the lesson.**

A team that does all four, even modestly, is doing harness engineering. A team that does only one or two is doing prompt engineering with extra steps.

State tells the system what it believes. Gates decide what it is allowed to do. Tools let it act. Verification checks what happened. Traces preserve the path. Each is a harness property. Each compounds. Each outlasts any particular model.

The model supplies capability. The harness preserves the lessons. The trace is how those lessons enter the system.

Thanks for reading the series.

## References

- Anthropic. "Building Effective Agents." December 2024. <https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents>
- Anthropic. "Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents." 2025. <https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents>
- Boeckeler, Birgitta. "The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy." InfoQ, May 2026. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_R83pFpUWyM>
- Iusztin, Paul. "Agentic Harness Engineering." Decoding AI, 2025. <https://www.decodingai.com/p/agentic-harness-engineering>
